items.title: A grief therapist told me I should live to honor my son. How would I do that?
items.blurb:
After my 22-month-old child died, music and poetry brought catharsis and comfort

The day Renzo had his aneurysm, in the afternoon before it happened, I distinctly remember telling him that it was going to be a pleasure to watch him grow up. It seems ridiculous to write that here, the foreshadowing so on the nose as to be unbelievable. But it’s true. To him and my wife Allegra, I said, “I love our little family.” After the struggles around his birth, job loss, the pandemic, it felt, finally, like we had made it to the other side. We had, I suppose, just not how I envisioned it.

My friends Jayson and Stacy recommended I get to know David Kessler’s work. According to Harvard Business Review, Kessler is “the world’s foremost expert on grief”. He’d worked closely with Elisabeth Kübler-­Ross, who was the reigning expert on grief before her own death in 2004. A large part of Kübler-­Ross’s legacy is her classification of what she calls the five stages of grief: denial, anger, bargaining, depression and acceptance, as described in her 1969 book On Death and Dying. After you experience a death, she wrote, these stages can occur in any order, with one or more possibly not occurring at all. Just weeks after Renzo’s death, I’d tricked myself into thinking I had jumped all the way to acceptance when really I was trying to outrun anger and depression. Bargaining hasn’t been a big one for me, nor has denial. The facts have been pretty hard to ignore.

items.title: The music in Nolan’s Odyssey is as well-crafted – and bloodlessly forgettable – as the film itself
items.blurb:
Ludwig Göransson’s meticulous score uses instruments of the era to create an earthy, raw and bleak soundscape, but it merely supports the portrayal of an uncomplicated hero

A few weeks ago I came across a video of Professor Armand D’Angour, associate professor of classics at Oxford university. He explained how, through groundbreaking research involving fragments of melody and rhythms found on papyrus and stone and the reconstruction of ancient instruments, it is now possible to hear for the first time in 2,000 years what ancient Greek music would have sounded like.

Given Ludwig Göransson’s beautiful theme for the aulos (an ancient double-barrelled flute) in Christopher Nolan’s latest blockbuster, The Odyssey, and his reputation for dedicated research into his subject matter, it seems very likely that he too has heard these calls from the ancient past.

items.title: The end of Heartstopper is a bittersweet loss – it’s never felt scarier to be young and queer | Alim Kheraj
items.blurb:
The gay Netflix romance maintained a political edge despite rollbacks to queer and trans rights

After six books, three seasons of television and a film-length finale, Heartstopper has come to an end. Often dubbed the most wholesome show on TV, Netflix’s adaptation of Alice Oseman’s young adult LGBTQ+ graphic novel series, which itself began life as a web comic, is a legitimate cultural phenomenon, turning its two leads – Kit Connor and Joe Locke – into household names, while racking up tens of millions of views. In print, meanwhile, the Heartstopper series has sold over 10m copies worldwide, including about 1.3m in the UK alone.

Yet these figures only tell one side of the story of Heartstopper’s impact. At the queer and feminist bookshop where I work part-time in Sheffield, the young people who come in are immediately drawn to copies of Oseman’s books, and often take pictures of the now permanent window display depicting Nick and Charlie, the couple at the story’s heart. I see young queer people’s faces light up when they see we have the book in stock, even though they probably already have a copy at home.

items.title: Air by Christian Kracht review – a metaphysical jigsaw puzzle
items.blurb:
A portal opens from our modern world of data centres and interior design into a medieval fantasy realm, in this playfully surreal tale from the author of Eurotrash

A reclusive interior designer named Paul travels from his home in Orkney to the Norwegian coastal city of Stavanger for a most unusual commission. A data­centre housing a vast internet server is located by a fjord on the outskirts of the city, and a prestigious arts magazine is paying him a large sum of money to give it a makeover. They want him to paint the interiors white – “not just any white, but the perfect white, the singular white”. Shortly after he arrives, a freak weather event causes a power outage, and Paul goes missing; meanwhile the magazine’s jaded editor, Cohen, takes an overdose of barbiturates.

In alternate chapters a very different tale unfolds, set in a distant medieval world redolent of Norse mythology. A man, referred to only as “the stranger”, is nursed back to health by a nine-year-old girl who has shot him with an arrow after mistaking him for a deer. He is being pursued by a vengeful duke, and persuades the girl to join him on the run. Together they leave their plague-ridden town, traverse a barren wasteland and discover a subterranean stone city whose inhabitants live a communal existence, subsisting on seafood and kelp.

items.title: Legal logging and soaring saplings: the ‘green islands’ bucking the deforestation trend in central America
items.blurb:
In Guatemala’s Maya biosphere reserve local people are making a living from trees at the same time as increasing forest cover, showing nature is best protected by those who depend on it

The low rumbling grows louder until a huge harvester crashes into the open, dragging a 15-metre trunk of mahogany in its wake. The tractor manoeuvres before releasing its load and shovelling it like a matchstick on to a growing pile of lumber at the edge of the clearing. Then, with a crunch of gears and a belch of diesel, it turns and lurches back into the forest.

This is sustainable forestry at its most brutal, yet in a few weeks’ time silence will return to this pocket of tropical forest in Guatemala’s Maya biosphere reserve (MBR). Nature will be left to reclaim the land and chainsaws won’t return for at least four years, as part of a strict management plan that is helping to buck the trend of rampant deforestation.

items.title: I am used to hot days now. But the warm wind has really panicked me | Adrian Chiles
items.blurb:
I first noticed it a couple of weeks ago on the streets of Cardiff – and it felt alarmingly wrong

When the wind is warm, you know something is up. Naturally, I have been well aware of the awful reality of the climate crisis, but it has taken the warmth of the wind to really panic me. Not the endless 30C+ days, the desiccated garden, the tap slowing to a drip. No, it is the warm wind blowing on me. I can just about manage in punishing heat, even though it is particularly suffocating when the air is still. But I thought I had a tacit agreement with the British climate that at least when there was a breeze it would cool me down a bit. When a breeze warms me up, here in the UK anyway, it feels nothing less than un-British, and I wonder if we really are getting into the end of days.

I first noticed it a couple of weeks ago on the streets of Cardiff. I turned a corner on St Mary’s Street and there it was, a strong gust of properly warm wind, right in my face and down into my lungs. Reeling, gasping a little, I looked around for the idling bus that had surely generated this unpleasant blast of air. But this wind hadn’t emanated from a bus’s rear, for there was no bus in sight. It was the weather and it felt alarmingly wrong.

items.title: Besieged by lobbyists, will UK ministers protect our ecosystems? No, they’ll chicken out | George Monbiot
items.blurb:
Industrial poultry farming is killing our rivers. But rather than stop it, the government has been facilitating it. Will Andy Burnham end this ecocide?

There’s a slightly mean question I often ask British people with an interest in the living world: can you name the environment secretary? I don’t intend to show them up: it’s not them I’m testing. I watch the brows knit, a finger go up, the mouth open and close. “It’s, you know, the one who … no, they went, didn’t they? That other one …” It’s been like this for seven years.

Why? Because throughout this period, we’ve not had environment secretaries. We’ve had portals through which corporate lobbyists may pass. The last incumbent to represent the wider public interest (and I can scarcely bring myself to type these words) was Michael Gove. In his other roles, he’s widely remembered with loathing and disgust. Out of office, he continues to poison public life, as editor of the Spectator. But in this role, to general astonishment, he placed the public good above special interests.

George Monbiot is a Guardian columnist

items.title: If you’ve heard that London is dystopian and dangerous – read this. It just isn’t | Sadiq Khan
items.blurb:
Britain's capital has faced social media, press and political attacks designed to make residents and tourists feel unsafe. Ignore that: we have the facts

Over the summer months, 5 million people will visit London, with its free museums and galleries, massive events featuring the biggest names in music and sport, and everything Britain’s capital city has to offer. And, as new figures released today show, they’ll be able to do so knowing London is one of the safest cities of its size in the world.

On social media, a dark blizzard of disinformation – much of it coordinated and amplified by extreme groups and hostile foreign actors – has seen bots and trolls depict London as a “lawless” city where crime goes unpunished and basic social decency has all but disappeared. But let’s deal in facts: data published by the Metropolitan police this morning shows that nothing could be further from the truth. Across the capital, there were 35,000 fewer “neighbourhood crimes” – a category which includes burglaries, thefts, robberies and vehicle crime offences – in the past 12 months than in the year before.

This has not happened by accident. As mayor I have provided a record £1.26bn in funding for the capital’s police force, more than double the amount under my predecessor. That funding has enabled it to invest in cutting-edge technology to keep pace with criminals, including ebikes and drones to pursue phone snatchers down London’s busy streets, but it has also boosted neighbourhood policing in communities across our city.

Now, we have more evidence that our approach is yielding real results. In Westminster – home to many communities and the centre of the capital’s tourism – where we’ve doubled the number of police officers and introduced a new “command cell” to lead a crackdown on phone theft and robbery, neighbourhood crime has fallen by 36%. In the West End, the beating heart of London’s visitor economy, phone thefts are down by half.

Since last year, thousands of police officers across our city have been arresting an extra 1,000 suspects every month on average. Their work has been tireless. But the figures also reflect the astonishing impact of the violence reduction unit (VRU), which I set up in 2019. When first elected mayor, I promised to be tough on crime and tough on the complex causes of crime. Through the VRU, we’ve done just that, providing interventions and positive opportunities to prevent more than half a million young people from getting caught up in a vicious cycle of violence and exploitation. And note this: since it was set up, hospital admissions of young people after knife assault, have fallen by almost 50%.

Sadiq Khan is mayor of London

The FCDO’s annual report was quietly released just as Starmer was leaving office – it continues a long-term shift from supporting countries most in need to seeking something in return

Somewhere on the new British prime minister’s desk sits a document. It was quietly published last week, in the dying hours of his predecessor’s government. The Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office’s annual report cements how the UK aid budget will be allocated until 2029.

It was released at the moment of least political scrutiny: parliament rising for the summer, one prime minister exiting Downing Street, the other waiting for the keys. Anyone asking whether the new PM, Andy Burnham, will – or even can – restore Britain’s 0.7% aid commitment should begin with this document, which appears designed to lock in three more years of retreat from the world’s poorest communities before Burnham could take office.

items.title: The Guardian view on Andy Burnham and devolution: it takes a powerful centre to decentralise power | Editorial
items.blurb:
When it comes to driving an agenda from No 10, the prime minister seems to have learned important lessons from his predecessor’s failure

Before entering Downing Street, Andy Burnham cited the overcentralisation of power as a cause of Britain’s social and economic malaise. In his first week, the new prime minister has signalled his intent to bolster No 10 as an organ of executive power. The apparent contradiction – fortifying the centre to pursue an agenda of decentralisation – arises from a perversity in the way the British state works or, rather, fails to work.

The prime minister is right that too much policy has historically been directed from Whitehall. Reforms in recent years have created institutions with varying degrees of local autonomy – elected mayoralties like the one in Manchester that afforded Mr Burnham a platform for national prominence. But genuine control, especially when it comes to fiscal policy, has not matched the rhetoric of devolution. The Treasury is wary of facilitating profligacy in lower tiers of government. Heads of devolved administrations are also adept at claiming credit when things go well and, when they go badly, diverting the blame on to national government.

items.title: The Guardian view on Chinese workers picking up the pen: poetry in motion | Editorial
items.blurb:
A food delivery rider has won the country’s highest literary award. It is part of a growing trend for writing by labourers

The hundreds of millions who have built modern China from the ground up – sweeping its streets, labouring in its factories and ferrying its goods – are often overlooked. But they refuse to go unheard. Last week, a food delivery rider won China’s top literary award. Wang Jibing, 56, first caught attention when a poem about his experiences went viral in 2022. The following year, Hu Anyan’s memoir, I Deliver Parcels in Beijing, became a bestseller.

In the early 20th century, Chinese intellectuals sought to capture the harsh realities of working-class lives. But it was under Mao Zedong that the idea of writing by as well as about the masses became reality, with labourers encouraged to take up the pen. In an era when the party tried to govern the language even of diaries and personal letters, the parameters of style and content were strict, and this enthusiasm swiftly diminished after Mao. But workers’ writing has seen a revival in the 21st century. This time, it is bottom-up rather than directed from above.

It is a theory that will shape his entire approach to life as the Liverpool head coach and his attempts to bring the connection, the joy and the belief back to Anfield. The Champions League, the Premier League, the Europa League, the FA Cup, the League Cup, the European Championship and, as of Sunday, the World Cup: all currently in the hands of Basque and Spanish coaches, even if Pep Guardiola has since taken his leave from Manchester City. items.title: Don’t like the result? Don’t worry, sport no longer ends on the field of play | Jonathan Liew
items.blurb:
From the wrong headset in an F2 garage to Fifa overturning a red card, we are in a landscape where fairness is the enemy

A funny and very small thing happened at the Formula Two race at Spa on Sunday. Noel León, of Campos Racing, carved his way through the field to finish seventh, earning himself six points in the drivers’ championship, and promptly left the circuit to go and do whatever it is Noel León likes to do after a race. Take a nap, hit the Belgian brews, congratulate himself on his palindromic name – whatever. Anyway, while he was doing that, more than three hours after the chequered flag, the FIA governing body called a meeting and decided to disqualify him.

The reason: a serious breach of appendix 5 and article 21.5 of the F2 Sporting Regulations – and assuming you don’t have those to hand, it’s the rule governing the use of headsets. During the race, a guest of León had been spotted in the Campos garage wearing what was deemed the wrong type of headset. According to the aforementioned appendix 5, only listening devices may be used by non-team personnel. This headset had a microphone, albeit upturned and unused.

items.title: For 50+ Years, Indians Could Fly the Tricolour Only on Select Days. One Man’s Fight Changed That
items.blurb: At his factory in Raigarh, then part of Madhya Pradesh, Naveen Jindal began flying the Indian Tricolour from his office premises in the early 1990s. It was a simple expression of pride, one he had grown accustomed to while studying in the United States. But in September 1994, the divisional commissioner of Bilaspur noticed the flag during a visit and objected. Under the Flag Code then in force, private citizens were generally allowed unrestricted display of the National Flag only on specified occasions. Jindal was warned that continuing to fly it could invite action. The Flag Code, however, was not an Act passed by Parliament. It consisted of executive instructions governing when and how the National Flag could be displayed. Jindal found the restriction difficult to accept. Years earlier, while studying abroad and serving as president of a university student senate in the United States, he had flown the Indian flag with pride among a sea of unfamiliar symbols. Returning home, he found it hard to accept that the same flag could not fly freely over his own soil , on his own building. Taking on the state He sought legal advice, including from senior advocate Shanti Bhushan. He was advised that two parliamentary laws directly regulated the use of the National Flag. The Emblems and Names (Prevention of Improper Use) Act, 1950, restricted its commercial misuse, while the Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act, 1971, penalised acts of disrespect. The Flag Code itself , his lawyers argued, did not have the force of legislation and could not independently take away a citizen’s constitutional freedom of expression. The restriction on everyday flag display came from executive instructions known as the Flag Code, not from a law passed by Parliament. Jindal then filed a writ petition before the Delhi High Court, challenging the restriction and asserting his right to fly the flag respectfully. In September 1995, the High Court ruled in his favour. It held that the Flag Code could not impose restrictions beyond those contained in valid laws enacted by Parliament. It seemed, briefly, that the matter was settled. But it had only just begun. The battle reaches the Supreme Court The Union of India appealed to the Supreme Court, arguing that letting citizens fly the flag freely was a matter of policy, not something for courts to decide. The appeal now carried a formal title, one that would define the next decade of the fight: Union of India versus Naveen Jindal. While the appeal remained pending , the Court asked the Union Government to examine whether the existing restrictions should be reconsidered. A committee was formed to review the Flag Code and the public’s right to display the Tricolour. The review led to the Flag Code of India, 2002, which came into effect on 26 January 2002. In 2021, India amended the Flag Code to permit machine-made and polyester National Flags alongside traditional hand-spun and handwoven versions. For the first time, members of the general public, private organisations and educational institutions were expressly permitted to display the National Flag on all days, provided they followed the rules protecting its honour and dignity. The 2002 Code had therefore lifted the practical restriction before the Supreme Court delivered its final judgment. The final word The verdict came on 23 January 2004, nearly a decade after the official objection in Raigarh. A bench led by then Chief Justice V N Khare held that respectfully flying the National Flag was a form of expression protected under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution. The Court clarified that this was a qualified fundamental right, subject to reasonable restrictions under Article 19(2) and the two parliamentary laws regulating the flag’s use and dignity. The judgment dismissed the Union Government’s appeal and gave constitutional recognition to a freedom that the 2002 Flag Code had already made available in practice. A campaign that had begun with one flag at a factory had reshaped how citizens could express their allegiance to the country. Beyond the courtroom Jindal later founded the Flag Foundation of India and continued campaigning for wider public awareness about the respectful display of the Tricolour. The rules evolved further over the years. In December 2021, the Flag Code was amended to permit machine-made and polyester flags alongside those made from hand-spun and handwoven materials . Since a 2022 amendment, the National Flag may remain hoisted both day and night if it is displayed in the open or at a private residence with due respect. In July 2022, another amendment allowed a National Flag displayed in the open or at a private residence to remain flown during both the day and night. Jindal has also advocated for 23 January, the date of the Supreme Court judgment, to be observed as National Flag Day. Today, the Tricolour flies over homes, shops and terraces across the country. It's raised without a second thought — in protests, rallies and elections that shape the nation's course — by citizens who carry it as a matter of course, not privilege. That everyday freedom exists because one man, on one factory roof, decided a flag was worth fighting for, all the way to India's highest court. Naveen Jindal ': CaseMine

items.title: This Forest Officer Wants to Turn Ujjain From a Concrete Jungle Back Into a Sacred Forest
items.blurb: Millions arrive in Ujjain every year with folded hands, seeking blessings at its ancient temples. They walk through sacred corridors and immerse themselves in a city whose spiritual legacy stretches back centuries. But few stop to wonder what Ujjain looked like before the temples. Long before it became one of India's holiest pilgrimage centres, ancient texts described it as Mahakal Van — a vast forest where nature and spirituality existed side by side. Over centuries, however, urban growth gradually replaced that green cover, leaving behind a city where open natural spaces are now few and far between. For Anurag Tiwari , an Indian Forest Service (IFS) officer from the 2018 Madhya Pradesh cadre and the Divisional Forest Officer of Ujjain, that forgotten past offers a roadmap for the city's future. "The first challenge was identifying land where greening could actually happen. Ujjain is an ancient city, and land availability is never easy," he says. Historically, Mahakal Van is believed to have spread across much of present-day Ujjain, which today covers between 51.83 and 157 sq km. Finding space, however, wasn't straightforward. Much of the city's open land falls under the Revenue Department rather than the Forest Department. So Tiwari and his team began searching for places where nature could return in a way that also resonated with people's faith. That search led them back to scripture. Recreating the sacred forest of Mahakal Van The Skanda Purana describes Ujjaini as the City of Mahakal Van. Many of the city's famous temples are believed to stand where shrines once existed within this sprawling forest. Drawing inspiration from these texts, the Forest Department is creating Aastha Vans — sacred groves along the Panchkoshi Yatra route. Each grove combines native tree species with plants traditionally associated with the deities worshipped in the region, bringing together ecology and faith. Long before it became one of India's holiest pilgrimage centres, ancient texts described it as Mahakal Van — a vast forest where nature and spirituality existed side by side. Photograph: ( Holidify ) The effort goes beyond planting saplings. The department is also trying to restore species that have quietly disappeared from Ujjain's landscape. Tamarind, once common, now survives only in scattered pockets. Miswak, which was once native to the Kshipra river basin, is now found mostly in southern India. "We are trying to develop Aastha Vans that can function as sacred groves while showcasing local biodiversity," says Tiwari. "The idea is to reconnect people with nature through the spiritual identity of Ujjain." This isn't Tiwari's first urban greening project. During his earlier posting in Khargone's Barwani division, he developed three biodiversity-focused urban forests, work that earned recognition from the World Book of Records. He is now applying those learnings to Ujjain. Turning communities into custodians Planting trees is only the beginning. Protecting them for decades requires local ownership. Instead of managing the groves entirely through the Forest Department, the plan is to hand each Aastha Van over to nearby temple trusts and Gram Panchayats. "The idea is that temple organisations and Gram Panchayats become the custodians of these sacred groves," says Tiwari. As one of India's busiest pilgrimage destinations, Ujjain sometimes becomes a transit point for banned wildlife products such as Hatha Jodi and Sehi Singhi, which are linked to wildlife crime and superstition. Photograph: ( Trawell ) The response has been encouraging, with plantation work already underway and strong community participation. To strengthen this connection , the department organised a six-day Van Mela earlier this year. The event brought together more than 60 Non-Timber Forest Produce (NTFP) cooperative societies, over 100 traditional healers and herbal brands. More than 1.5 lakh people visited the fair, while herbal product sales crossed Rs 1 crore , demonstrating how conservation can also support local livelihoods. Protecting more than just trees The department's work extends beyond restoring forests. As one of India's busiest pilgrimage destinations, Ujjain sometimes becomes a transit point for banned wildlife products such as Hatha Jodi and Sehi Singhi , which are linked to wildlife crime and superstition. To tackle this, the Forest Department has stepped up surveillance, carrying out four to five major enforcement drives in a single month. At the same time, it is developing a 210-hectare naturalistic zoo on reserved forest land. The project has already received in-principle approval from the Central Zoo Authority and forms part of the city's broader green vision ahead of the Ujjain Simhastha Kumbh Mela 2028 . For Tiwari, however, the heart of the effort lies in the Aastha Vans. If the sacred groves flourish, native species return and local communities continue to care for them, Ujjain could once again become a city where faith and forests grow together. His vision is simple: not just to plant more trees, but to help one of India's oldest sacred cities rediscover the forest that once gave it its identity. items.title: How a Rescued Street Dog Became Uttarakhand Police's First K9 Hero
items.blurb: Most police dogs in the world are carefully bred, selected from established bloodlines, and trained for months to detect explosives, track suspects or locate missing people. India too followed the same process for a very long time. But Thenga's story was different. He was a thin, young stray wandering near the police lines in Dehradun when Uttarakhand Police decided to take a chance on him. There was no pedigree certificate, no specialised breeding programme and no expectation that he would one day stand alongside German Shepherds and Labradors in the state's elite dog squad. A few years later, Thenga became one of the strongest arguments for why India's security agencies are beginning to rethink what makes a good working dog. A chance that changed everything In 2019, the Uttarakhand Police launched an experiment, wherein instead of recruiting another imported breed, officers from the Bomb Detection and Disposal Squad (BDDS) adopted a rescued Indian street dog and began training him as a sniffer canine. They named him Thenga. Thenga is the colloquial Hindi word often associated with defiance or breaking convention. The name turned out to be fitting. Picked up as a puppy by handler Kamlesh Pant, Thenga spent his first month under Pant's care before entering formal police training. During an obstacle drill at the Dehradun police lines, the eight-month-old dog surprised trainers and onlookers alike. View this post on Instagram A post shared by The Better India (@thebetterindia) While pedigree working dogs often require several months to master advanced obedience and agility exercises, Thenga grasped commands with unusual speed. He cleared five-foot jumps, threaded through hoops and demonstrated precise scent-tracking skills that trainers usually expect only after extended practice. He responded exceptionally well to training, often matching, and in some drills outperforming dogs from breeds long considered the gold standard for police work. His agility was only one part of the story. Handlers also found that Thenga adapted well to local conditions, remained active through long training sessions and was less susceptible to the seasonal illnesses that sometimes affect imported breeds . Eventually, Thenga started serving as a trained tracking, rescue and sniffer dog in Dehradun, assisting in tasks ranging from detecting explosives and narcotics to tracking suspects and helping locate missing persons. More than one dog's success Thenga's journey is important because it challenges a standard that has shaped India's security forces for decades. For years, police units, the armed forces and disaster-response agencies largely depended on imported breeds such as German Shepherds, Belgian Malinois and Labradors. These dogs came with internationally recognised training standards and long histories of military and police work. But experiences on the ground have prompted a reassessment. And security agencies started recognising that dogs born and raised in India are naturally suited to the country's climate, terrain and disease environment. They tolerate heat better, often require lower veterinary care, possess strong endurance and are capable of working for long hours in challenging conditions. Dogs born and raised in India are naturally suited to the country's climate, terrain and disease environment. They tolerate heat better, often require lower veterinary care, possess strong endurance and are capable of working for long hours in challenging conditions. Photograph: (AFP/Getty Images) That shift has now expanded beyond one rescued stray in Uttarakhand. The Indian Army has inducted the Mudhol Hound, Rampur Hound, Kombai, Gaddi, Bakharwal, and Tibetan Mastiff on a trial basis for patrol and surveillance duties. The Central Reserve Police Force (CRPF) has also evaluated these indigenous breeds for tracking and operational roles, while several state police forces and disaster-response agencies have broadened programmes involving native Indian dogs. But not every Indian dog is the same Thenga's story also needs a conversation about the distinction between India's native breeds and its street dogs. Indigenous breeds such as the Mudhol Hound, Rampur Hound, Kombai, Rajapalayam and Pandikona have been selectively adapted over centuries for hunting, guarding or herding. But street dogs, or Indies, as they are often called are different. Most have mixed ancestry rather than belonging to a standardised breed. Their value lies in individual qualities: temperament, intelligence, health and trainability. Thenga represents this second category. Thenga really did prove that in some cases the most capable recruit is not the one bred for the job, but the one finally given the opportunity. Photograph: (Image enhanced with AI) His success was not because every stray can automatically become a police dog. It was because trainers recognised potential in one animal and invested time in developing it. That approach is also beginning to be accepted. Rescued Indie dogs have already demonstrated their capabilities in search-and-rescue missions, explosive detection and policing. Their resilience, scenting ability and adaptation to Indian conditions have made them equally valuable partners in disaster response and law enforcement. Redefining who gets a chance There is a reason videos of Thenga racing through obstacle courses became viral during 2019 when Uttarakhand Police shared them. People were watching long-held assumptions being questioned. For decades, the image of a police dog in India has been tied to imported breeds. Thenga's story suggested that capability cannot always be predicted by lineage. As more agencies evaluate native breeds and well-trained Indies for specialised roles, the country's working-dog programmes are becoming less dependent on imported bloodlines and more reflective of India's own landscapes. Thenga really did prove that in some cases the most capable recruit is not the one bred for the job, but the one finally given the opportunity. Sources: ‘Stray dog 'Thenga' breaks tradition, opens way to end Pedigree monopoly in armed forces’ : by The New Indian Express, Published on 20 Nov 2019 ‘Tangkhul Hui and Kombai breeds to add bite to the dog squad of Assam Rifles’ : by Rahul Karmakar, Published on 13 Feb 2026

items.title: Panna Lost All Its Tigers. How Science & the Ken River Powered Its Historic Comeback
items.blurb: Recent headlines have brought Panna Tiger Reserve back into the spotlight. The reason is the Ken-Betwa River Link Project , whose proposed Daudhan Dam is expected to submerge thousands of hectares of forest within one of India's best-known tiger landscapes. The discussion goes beyond the fate of a single reserve. It raises questions about the future of one of India's greatest wildlife recovery stories. Just over 15 years ago, Panna had lost all its tigers. Through one of the country's most ambitious conservation efforts, the reserve rebuilt its population from scratch. Today, it is home to around 80 tigers. Now, conservationists are asking a new question: what happens when a landscape painstakingly restored over decades begins to change? Because bringing back tigers was only one part of Panna's recovery. Healthy forests, abundant prey, connected habitats and reliable water sources all played an equally important role. Scientists say that when any of these ecological building blocks are altered, the effects can ripple across the entire ecosystem. When Panna lost its Tigers For decades, Panna was considered ideal tiger country. The reserve, spread across the forests of Madhya Pradesh, was once the hunting ground of the princely states of Panna, Chhatarpur and Bijawar. It became a national park in 1981 and was later declared a tiger reserve in 1994. Over the years, adjoining forests and buffer areas expanded the protected landscape to nearly 1,600 sq km. On paper, everything seemed in place. Yet poaching quietly undid years of conservation. Dominated by the Ken River running through its center, the terrain creates perfect natural corridors and riverbank vantage points for tigers to hunt and thrive. Photograph: (Mehak Noni) During the early 2000s, Panna supported an estimated 24 to 40 tigers. But by 2009, only one lone male tiger remained. The reserve had lost the very species it was created to protect. For conservationists, it was more than the disappearance of a predator. It marked the collapse of an entire ecological process. What followed became one of India's most closely watched wildlife recovery programmes. The comeback that few thought was possible In June 2009, the Madhya Pradesh Forest Department, with support from WWF and the Panna Tiger Reserve management, began an ambitious tiger reintroduction programme. The first arrivals were two tigresses — T1 from Bandhavgarh and T2 from Kanha. They were soon joined by a male tiger, T3, from Pench. Soon after his release, T3 began walking back towards Pench, travelling through villages and other human-dominated landscapes without triggering conflict. Forest officials tracked him continuously for more than a month before bringing him back to Panna in December 2009. This time, he stayed. He established a territory, found mates and became one of the founders of Panna's recovering population . In April 2010, T1 gave birth to four cubs. A few months later, T2 also produced four cubs. Two more tigresses, T4 and T5, were introduced in 2011. Although T4 later died from an infection caused by her radio collar, the programme had already gained momentum. The reserve is characterized by a "table-top" topography with parallel plateaus, deep gorges, and grassy meadows. This dynamic terrain creates micro-habitats that support a high density of prey species like sambar, chital, and nilgai. Photograph: (Benchmark3/Wikipedia) Over the years, seven translocated tigers — five females and two males — produced more than 80 cubs. What had once seemed impossible became one of India's biggest conservation successes. From having no breeding population in 2009, Panna's tiger numbers rose to 54 by 2019. In 2020, UNESCO recognised the reserve by including it in the World Network of Biosphere Reserves. Today, Panna is estimated to support around 80 tigers. It was never just about moving Tigers The remarkable recovery is documented in Return of the King: How Panna National Park Got Its Tigers Back , directed by Mandakini Malla and produced by Roundglass Sustain . The documentary shows that relocating tigers was only the beginning. Every breeding tiger was fitted with a radio collar, allowing forest teams to monitor their movements around the clock. Rangers tracked dispersal, breeding and territorial behaviour, helping ensure the animals adapted safely to their new home. But the film also highlights a larger lesson. Conservation biologist Dr Raghu Chundawat, who has studied tigers in Panna for decades, says the reserve reflects the challenges facing India's dry forests. These landscapes make up nearly half of the country's tiger habitat but are among those where tiger populations have declined the fastest. The reason, he explains, is what he calls a "scale mismatch". Unlike tigers living in denser forests, those in dry forests need territories three to four times larger because prey is spread out over wider areas. As roads, settlements and infrastructure fragment these forests, sustaining healthy tiger populations becomes increasingly difficult. For Chundawat, Panna and Sariska serve as important reminders that protecting habitat is just as critical as protecting the animals themselves. The documentary also features wildlife filmmaker Joanna Van Gruisen, who says lasting conservation depends on local communities. Families living around Panna rely on farming and forest resources for their livelihoods. When conservation also supports their well-being, protecting wildlife becomes a shared goal rather than a competing interest. Why the Ken River matters Panna's recovery was never only about bringing back tigers. It was about restoring an entire ecosystem. Researchers from the Wildlife Institute of India (WII) and the Zoological Survey of India (ZSI), in their study Interspecific Interaction among Mammals in Panna Tiger Reserve, Central India: Prey-Predator Dynamics, found that water plays a central role in shaping the reserve's wildlife. Their research showed that the likelihood of detecting mammals declined as the distance from water increased. As WII researcher Supratim Dutta told Mongabay, "The Ken River functions as the ecological backbone of the entire mammalian community." The study suggests that Panna's recovery succeeded because several ecological pieces came together: healthy prey populations, connected forests, careful management and dependable water sources. In other words, restoring predators alone is not enough. The landscape that sustains them must also remain healthy. Even before the current debate, scientists had examined how future development could affect the reserve. A 2021 paper published in Current Science, titled The Inter-linking of Rivers and Biodiversity Conservation: A Study of Panna Tiger Reserve, estimated that the Ken-Betwa project could directly submerge about 58.03 sq km, roughly 10% of the reserve's critical tiger habitat. The study also projected an indirect loss of more than 105 sq km because of habitat fragmentation and reduced connectivity. These studies underline an important lesson from Panna's remarkable comeback. The reserve's revival did not happen simply because a few tigers were relocated. It happened because forests remained connected, prey populations recovered, water continued to sustain the landscape through harsh summers, and tigers had enough space to establish territories and raise cubs. In Panna, the future of the tiger has always been closely tied to the future of the landscape it calls home. Sources: ‘Interspecific Interaction among Mammals in Panna Tiger Reserve, Central India: Prey-Predator Dynamics’ : by Supratim Dutta, Gopinathan Maheswaran, K. items.title: China’s new chip stores data with a single electron, breaking AI memory bottleneck
items.blurb: How many electrons does it take to store a single bit of information? For today’s most advanced dynamic random access memory (DRAM) chips from Samsung and SK Hynix, the answer is roughly 200,000. That is the price of reliability – a vast reservoir of charge needed to ensure a “1” or “0” does not fade into noise. items.title: I Grow Figs, Grapes, Passion Fruits, Pineapples & 5 Types of Mangoes on My Terrace
items.blurb: Originally reported and written in August 2022, this story has been republished as part of our archival content. Elizabeth Kota and her family moved to their residence in Hyderabad amid the first lockdown in 2020. For the gardening enthusiast Elizabeth, it was the perfect time to commence her farming activities on a 1200-square feet terrace. “When we finally moved to our own house from a congested rented apartment, I found space to put together a terrace garden, which was always a dream. During the lockdown, when there was a sudden break from professional engagements, I decided to make the most of the time,” says the 47-year-old who owns a cookware business. Elizabeth says she was always aware of the adverse effects of eating store-bought vegetables and fruits which not only ruin one’s health but also lack taste. “Due to the use of chemicals, most of our children don’t know the true taste of fruits. This is the reason why I decided to plant fruits . Today, I grow grapes, figs, apples, chikoo, mosambis (sweet limes), oranges, lemons, bananas, watermelons, pineapples, five varieties of mangoes and many more,” she adds. Some of the fruits from Eli's World. She started gardening by planting seasonal vegetables like okras, brinjals, tomatoes, leafy vegetables, cucumbers and a variety of creepers, among others. When this became a success, she didn’t have to buy any vegetables apart from onions and potatoes from stores. This gave her the confidence to move into fruit farming in pots. She started with chikoos, mangoes and grapes . Later, she found many varieties of these fruits and planted them too. Elizabeth says her gardening success was attributed to the ‘right’ potting mix used. She mixes red soil, river sand, cow dung and neem cake in a 3:3:3:1 ratio. As fertilisers, she uses kitchen compost and vermicompost along with cow dung. The pesticides used are 5 ml cow urine mixed in one-litre water or diluted neem oil. This is to be sprayed once every 15 days. “I take extra care to use organic manure and pesticides. Otherwise, what is the difference between store-bought and homemade produce?” The harvest from her terrace is majorly used in the household and distributed among family and friends. At present nothing is sold commercially but she plans to take some land on lease and start organic farming. “There are many people who wish to switch to organic farm produce but don't find time to grow it. My primary customer base will be them,” she shares. For those interested in organic farming, the urban gardener says, “There is no need to spend a lot of money or space to begin organic farming. You can even put together a garden on a 100-square feet terrace for less than Rs 1,000.” Elizabeth's terrace garden. “Since I plant a lot of varieties in more than 300 pots, I have incurred an expense of Rs 30,000 to date. The same can be done in grow bags, which costs less,” she adds. She also adds that if a considerable amount of time is spent setting up the garden, it takes less than an hour per day to take care of them. “For me, plants are like my kids, so I visit them once in two hours,” she says joyfully. Other than farming, Elizabeth gives lectures as a part of the horticulture department which trains new urban farmers. She helps families set up their terrace gardens. “I charge Rs 25,000 for setting up 40 grow bags. This includes potting mix, fertiliser, seeds and pesticides. I also visit the garden twice to check the growth, under the package,” she says. Elizabeth is also the admin of four WhatsApp groups that are filled with farming enthusiasts who have plenty of doubts. In the same year when she started gardening, Elizabeth also found time to begin a YouTube channel called Eli’s World , where she shares her farming techniques, tips and tricks. Her videos have a viewership of 5,000-10,000. “It was through YouTube that I studied farming in detail. So, I started the channel for people like me to study farming easily,” she gushes. Elizabeth in her garden. Elizabeth hopes to start teaching farming formally as a crash course to beginners. “It is important to have healthy produce daily and this can only be possible if one owns a small piece of garden in their respective homes. I vouch for that and will extend support to those interested,” says the urban gardener. Edited by Yoshita Rao

items.title: He Found a War Veteran Sleeping on Streets. His Work Now Helps 350+ Defence Families Rebuild Life
items.blurb: His father served 28 years in the Indian Army. Gaurav Shukla (36), never made it past the selection process himself. A decade later, he has built an organisation that quietly reaches what the system does not — non-pensioner veterans over the age of 60, war widows without income, soldiers living in unfortunate conditions within their own local communities in the remote parts of India. In 2017, a photograph of an elderly man near Connaught Place in Delhi went viral on social media. The man — later identified as Peethambaran — was hunched on a footpath, one arm resting on a crutch, an iron rod inserted in his hip joint after a rooftop accident months earlier. Cricketer Gautam Gambhir had shared the image, and the comments section quickly filled with assumptions: that the man was a drunk, a burden, someone who had brought this upon himself. In 2017, a viral photograph of an elderly man near Connaught Place drew public judgment from strangers who had no idea he was a war veteran. What the commenters did not know about Peethambaran is that he served the Indian Army as a Signalman in the Signal Corps during the 1965 and 1971 wars. He decided to leave his uniform behind to return to Keralam and take care of his terminally ill mother. Following this break, he had fallen short of the 15-year service threshold that was needed to qualify for a pension. “After my mother's death, my family was scattered, and I had no reason to stay. I came to Delhi and spent the next four decades surviving on whatever work I could find while I was young. The situation got worse after the building accident in 2017,” he told The Better India. Peethambaran had even made three attempts to end his life owing to loneliness, depression and financial instability. Gaurav Shukla, 26 at the time, saw that photograph and decided to go find him. A soldier's son who never got to serve Gaurav grew up in a household shaped by military life. His father wore the army's uniform for 28 years and served during the 1999 Kargil War. Gaurav tried multiple times to join the armed forces himself and did not make it. The loss stayed with him. Gaurav attempted the armed forces selection process more than once and never made it through, a loss that stayed with him long after. “The uniform, to me, represented something not just duty, but a kind of pride I had grown up watching from close range and had never quite been able to claim,” says Gaurav. He built a different career instead, completing a master's in TV production, broadcast, and journalism, then moving to Mumbai, working in film casting, eventually handling auditions for television serials. He was good at it. He also had, somewhere in the background, a different kind of restlessness. The Jantar Mantar turning point In 2015, he visited Jantar Mantar during the One Rank One Pension movement and found what he later described as a "mini Bharat" — veterans from Rajasthan, Haryana, Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, the Northeast, gathered in a protest that national television was handling with selective accuracy. In 2015, a visit to Jantar Mantar during the One Rank One Pension protests showed Gaurav a gap between what the news reported and what veterans were actually living through. His father watched the news at home and was told that pension arrears had been distributed. The men at the protest site described a different reality. The gap between what was being reported and what was actually true became, for Gaurav, a threshold he could not step back from. First cases, first name He made a video about three soldiers from a Haryana village who had collectively fought in the wars of 1962, 1965, and 1971. A colonel's wife shared it on social media. Upon seeing the video, a Businessman from Jaipur reached out and offered to give Rs 5,000 to Sepoy Banarasi (one of the 3 soldiers whose story was shot) as a token of appreciation and gratitude. “Alongside that, I also came to know of and reached my first real case — a martyr's family in Faizabad — when I cold-called the Sarpanchs of several villages in North India, trying to find out if there were families of any ex-soldiers living in distress who needed support,” Gaurav says. A video about three soldiers who had fought in the wars of 1962, 1965, and 1971 led to Gaurav's first donation case and, soon after, the founding of Support Our Heroes. For the Faizabad soldier's family, the team was able to travel to Faizabad to meet them, and persuaded the school to waive the tuition fee for the children through the twelfth grade. “Soon, one family led to another and the cycle began,” he recalls. He named the organisation Support Our Heroes (SOH) , which was established around February 2017. Its stated purpose was simple: to connect a need with someone who has the resources to meet it. The Ex-soldier sleeping at Connaught Place In 2019, when Gaurav came across the photograph of Peethambaran, he spent close to a week trying to locate him. He printed an A3-sized copy of the image and walked the Connaught Place area systematically, showing it to shopkeepers, guards, and vendors. A security guard outside a jewellery store eventually pointed him in the right direction. Garav recalls that when he visited his home, what he found was a structure made of sheets, plastic, and rope — the kind that people in long-term homelessness build out of whatever the city discards. Peethambaran was around 75 at the time and his hearing had deteriorated. Decades earlier, Peethambaran served as a Signalman in the Signal Corps during the 1965 and 1971 wars, before leaving service to nurse his dying mother — a decision that cost him his pension. The metal rod that had been inserted in his hip joint during a surgery because of which he was using crutches to walk with support. He was drinking, and he told Gaurav plainly that it was not out of happiness. He had tried to die three times. The rope had broken each time. “I sat with him for an hour. Forty minutes of that conversation were spent with Peethambaran weeping without pause. My heart ached to watch him like that but I had to do my duty and comfort him. What he needed most then was support and a shoulder to simply cry on,” Gaurav says. Building consistency, one payment at a time The first thing Gaurav addressed was health. The second was consistency. Support Our Heroes began covering rations and medicines through a vendor payment model that has since become the organisation's standard practice: rather than putting cash in a beneficiary's hands, SOH pays the local grocery store and the pharmacy directly via QR transfer each month. “Peethambaran walks in, collects what he needs, and the bill goes back to the organisation. There is no handling of money, no risk of the funds being spent elsewhere, and for an 82-year-old with intermittent cognitive clarity, no anxiety about managing a transaction,” Gaurav emphasises. Once support began, SOH started paying local shops and pharmacies directly each month, so Peethambaran never had to handle cash himself. He stopped drinking entirely — a transformation Gaurav mentions with particular care, knowing where Peethambaran was when they first met. “He now reads the philosophical writings of Swami Vivekananda to fill the hours, takes whatever walk his body allows in the mornings, and makes himself tea,” Gaurav mentions. A life steadied On his wall, Peethambaran keeps a piece of paper with Gaurav's phone number written on it. He explained the reasoning himself: " Kal agar main mar jaata hoon, toh koi bataane waala nahin hai. Koi soonghta hua aayega, phone number dekhega, call kar dega. (If I die tomorrow, there is no one to inform. At least if someone passing by sees the number, they will make the call).” He carries a matching card in his pocket, tucked alongside his ex-serviceman identity document. SOH has placed him in old age homes three times. Each time, he has come back within days. A man who has lived outdoors for four decades, Gaurav observed, cannot simply be settled into a closed room. Peethambaran put his own reasoning plainly: if he lives alone, at least his troubles are his own. Seeing others suffer would only add to his suffering. In this, there is something that resembles a clear-eyed dignity. The ex-serviceman's Penury Grant case has been filed with the appropriate office but remains in the queue. SOH continues bridging the gap — somewhere between four and seven thousand rupees a month, depending on what arises — while following up on the pending paperwork. The widow who carried stones In the hills of Garhwal in Uttarakhand, Roshni Pande had been living a different kind of quiet endurance. In Uttarakhand, Roshni Pande was left to raise her daughter alone after her husband was discharged short of his pension and later passed away — she turned to manual labour under MGNREGA, earning as little as Rs 150–200 a fortnight. Her husband, Sapper Digresh Kumar Pande, served in the Indian Army for 13.5 years before a mental illness due to which he was known to be aggressive and often clashed with his officers, which led to multiple warnings and, eventually, his discharge from service on disciplinary grounds. Being relieved from service without pension — especially when he was so close to completing the 15 years required for pensionable service — further worsened his mental health. He fell ill and eventually passed away. He died before reaching the 15-year threshold that would have entitled his family to a pension. Roshni was 42 when it happened. Their daughter was around 14 years of age. Years of manual labour “To keep the household going, I enrolled under MGNREGA — the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act scheme, which offers manual labour work on rural public infrastructure projects. For me, this meant road construction: carrying and laying stones by hand for six hours a day, waiting several months for payment to arrive in my bank account,” recalls Roshni, with a tinge of sorrow in her tone. The rate was between Rs 150 and Rs 200 for a fortnight of work, and the scheme was available for only two to three months of the year. “The remaining nine months I managed through vegetable farming and group work in the village. A separate NGO engaged with my case for six months but unfortunately was not able to produce anything,” she says. “Her case was then proposed to me by an Ex-Sergeant from Uttarakhand, asking if he could get something done for her. SOH hadn't been formed yet, and I was still an individual who used to visit Jantar Mantar regularly, and therefore, was in touch with Ex-Defence officers there,” Gaurav says. After being approached to help Roshni Pandey, he connected her with an Ex-Servicemen Welfare organisation run by some ex-officers. She engaged with the organisation for about six months and followed up multiple times, but nothing came of it — she was simply asked to wait each time she reached out. A shop, then a business The endless waiting prompted Gaurav to step in and help her in his individual capacity. He asked her what she would like to do for a living, so she could start leading a dignified life. “I told him that I wanted to run a shop selling puja items — the ritual goods that are consistent household spending across Garhwal. I estimated needing fifty to sixty thousand rupees to stock it. SOH gave me sixty thousand,” Roshni says, but this time, feeling gratitude and a sense of pride in her work. The shop worked. Monthly earnings settled between eight and ten thousand rupees. But Roshni did not stop there . She added mushroom farming. Then, papad is manufactured using a machine and a dryer to produce lentil crackers. Then pickles. Then, additional grinding equipment. A one-time grant of Rs 60,000 helped Roshni open a small shop, which she eventually grew into a multi-venture business employing other widows in her village. She now employs two other women from similarly difficult circumstances — widows — and pays each of them Rs 3,000 to Rs 4,000 a month. Recognition and a message to others The recognition arrived in stages. Along with Gaurav, All India Radio invited her to share her story on air. She was felicitated at Garhwal Bhavan in Delhi for women's empowerment. The district magistrate's office in Pauri acknowledged her work. When the radio programme aired, her phone rang for days. Neighbours, acquaintances, people from her village block, all calling to say they had heard her. " Roshni, tu toh star ban gayi re (Roshini you have become a star!)," she says, with a laugh. Her message to other defence families left without support is consistent: sitting still, she says, only brings more darkness. You have to move forward on your own courage, because that part, no one else can do for you. The gap between service and support The two stories are connected by something more precise than misf ortune. Both Peethambaran and Roshni's husband fell short of the 15-year continuous service threshold that determines pension eligibility. SOH was built on a specific principle: it does not duplicate what the government already provides — it exists only in the space the state doesn't reach. Both were consequently excluded from ECHS — the Ex-Servicemen Contributory Health Scheme — which provides medical coverage to veterans. Both existed, for all formal purposes, outside the welfare structure built for those who serve. India retires approximately 50,000 soldiers every year . A significant proportion fall below this threshold and leave the forces without a pension, without medical coverage, and often without proximity to welfare offices or the documentation support needed to navigate what little bureaucratic provision exists. “Support Our Heroes was built specifically for this population. We are deliberate on the point: the organisation does not replicate what the state provides. It fills the spaces the state does not reach,” Gaurav explains with care. Over the past ten years — Gaurav started this work at 26 and is now 36 — SOH has supported more than 350 soldiers and their families, with over 140 beneficiaries receiving lifetime monthly support across 22 states through its various projects. Six projects, one mission Six focused projects form the operational structure that makes this possible: Project PITHU delivers monthly ration kits to non-pensioner veterans and their widows above 65. Project SEHAT covers ongoing medications for non-pensioner veterans and their widows above 60 years of age a month for those excluded from ECHS. Project SAKSHAM pays school fees through the twelfth grade for children of deceased or disabled veterans. Project SASHAKT funds small business for Disabled soldiers (post-retirement), widows/veer nari, non-pensioner needy Ex-soldiers & widows, and dependents. starts, from Rs 8,000 for a sewing machine to Rs 1 lakh for a grocery shop, with ventures running in Manipur, Chhattisgarh, and Assam. A memorial scholarship in the name of Wing Commander Vinod — a Two times Vir Chakra Awardee from 1965 and 1971 Indo-Pak wars, who was part of SOH's core team before his death — supports medically boarded-out cadets & PBORs from Training Academies. Project INSANIYAT covers everything that falls outside the others: emergency cancer treatment bills, disaster relief operations across Poonch, Punjab, Manipur, and Uttarakhand. The six projects aren't separate causes, they're one life's needs, broken into pieces small enough to fund. The organisation has received recognition from 12 Governors, five Chief Ministers, various departments of the Ministry of Defence (MoD) and recently from the Hon’ble President of India. 'A soldier without a uniform' Although Gaurav Shukla could not become a soldier in the army, he proudly describes himself as “a soldier without a uniform”. The uniform represented pride, he says. He never got to wear it but this is his way of contributing to the people of the force. And on a more conscious note, the funding model is deliberately distributed: some serving officers contribute on a monthly, quarterly, or annual cycle; retired military personnel give what they can; civilian supporters and defence families participate; and small self-help groups pool together for specific cases. Each case is cross-verified through a network of serving officer course-mates posted across regions, alongside district sainik boards and village-level Ex-servicemen committees. When a case is resolved, documentation and footage of the outcome go back to everyone who contributed,” Gaurav explains. A guiding principle The principle guiding the organisation is: If someone in need comes to you and you cannot help them, never simply say sorry and send them away. Tell them where else to go. This was the guiding principle from Gen KM Cariappa (The First Indian Army Commander-in-Chief, Later Field Marshal). Fifty thousand soldiers retire every year, and a fraction of them vanish from the system entirely — SOH's real achievement is refusing to let that be someone else's problem. “SOH has also designed its projects in such a way that if a soldier family in need comes to us, we are able to help them in the best possible manner,” says Gaurav. It is the simplest possible formulation of what humane welfare looks like. And it is the opposite of what Peethambaran encountered for four decades before someone came with a printed photograph and a willingness to walk the streets of Connaught Place asking strangers if they had seen this man. “I am 82 now, or somewhere around that. I live alone, by choice. I read in the mornings, walk when my body cooperates, and keep the number on my wall,” Peethambaran says, happy to just have someone to talk to for a brief period over a phone call. In Garhwal, Roshni Pande rises early, runs her machines, checks on the women she employs, and manages a cluster of small businesses that did not exist seven years ago. Both of them, in different ways, are proof of what becomes possible when someone decides that the gap in the welfare net is not a fixed feature of the world. items.title: From Mumbai's Footpaths to Global Stardom, the Struggle-to-Success Story of Mithun Chakraborty
items.blurb: Whether you know him as the energetic "Grandmaster" from Dance India Dance or the man who made an entire generation groove to Disco Dancer , Mithun Chakraborty has remained a beloved name for decades. But behind the iconic dance moves, blockbuster films and countless accolades lies a story few know in its entirety. From walking away from a dangerous past and sleeping on Mumbai's footpaths while chasing an acting career to becoming one of India's most celebrated actors with over 350 films and three National Film Awards, Mithun's journey is one of perseverance, reinvention and determination. A difficult beginning Born as Gouranga Chakraborty in Kolkata, Mithun spent his childhood far removed from the glamour of the silver screen. He completed his BSc in Chemistry from Scottish Church College, but life soon took an unexpected turn. During the politically turbulent years of the late 1960s and early 1970s, he became associated with the Naxalite movement. However, the accidental death of his only brother became a turning point in his life. Realising he wanted a different future, Mithun decided to leave the movement despite the risks involved. The decision wasn't easy, but it marked the beginning of a new chapter. Before the fame, awards and iconic dance moves, there was a young man who fought rejection, hardship and uncertainty to find his place in cinema. Photograph: ( Variety ) Determined to rebuild his life, he enrolled at the Film and Television Institute of India (FTII) in Pune, where he began learning the craft of acting and discovered a new purpose. An award-winning debut, but no easy road Mithun's talent became evident from the very start. In 1976, he made his film debut with acclaimed filmmaker Mrinal Sen's Mrigayaa . Playing a tribal hunter, he delivered a performance that earned him the National Film Award for Best Actor—a rare achievement for a newcomer. Yet, winning India's highest acting honour did not instantly open doors. Like countless aspiring actors who arrived in Mumbai with dreams of making it big, Mithun struggled to find work. According to several reports, there were days when he slept on footpaths because he couldn't afford a place to stay. He also faced discrimination because of his dark complexion at a time when Bollywood favoured a very different image of a leading man. Instead of letting rejection define him, Mithun persisted. He took on whatever opportunities came his way, patiently waiting for the breakthrough that would change his life. The rise of India's 'Disco King' That breakthrough arrived with Surakksha (1979), where audiences embraced him as the stylish action hero 'Gunmaster G9'. The film established him as a bankable commercial actor, but an even bigger success was waiting just around the corner. In 1982 came Disco Dancer —the film that transformed Mithun into a cultural phenomenon. With Disco Dancer, Mithun didn’t just create a hit film, he created a cultural movement that made millions dance across borders. Photograph: ( The Indian Express ) Songs like I Am a Disco Dancer became instant hits, while his dance moves inspired millions across the country. The film's popularity wasn't limited to India. It became a blockbuster in the Soviet Union and several Asian countries, making Mithun one of the earliest Indian actors to enjoy an international fan following. At a time when Bollywood heroes were often defined by conventional looks, Mithun carved his own identity through confidence, talent and an unmistakable style that audiences instantly connected with. Reinventing himself time and again Unlike many stars who remained confined to mainstream Bollywood, Mithun wasn't afraid to take risks. In the 1990s, he shifted base to Ooty and established Monarch Studios. From there, he produced and starred in several low-budget action films that resonated deeply with audiences in smaller towns and single-screen theatres. While they rarely made headlines in metropolitan cities, these films helped him build an incredibly loyal fan base across India. From the silver screen to television’s beloved ‘Grandmaster’, Mithun Chakraborty reinvented himself with every era and won new generations of fans. Photograph: ( The Hindu ) Even during this phase, he continued proving his versatility as an actor. His performances in Tahader Katha (1992) and Swami Vivekananda (1998) earned him two more National Film Awards, reaffirming that he could excel in both commercial and critically acclaimed cinema. Becoming television's beloved 'Grandmaster' As Indian entertainment evolved, so did Mithun. In the 2000s, he successfully transitioned to television as the "Grandmaster" on Dance India Dance . His warmth, humility and genuine encouragement towards contestants made him a favourite among viewers, introducing him to an entirely new generation that knew him as much for his kindness as his stardom. Beyond entertainment, Mithun also stepped into public life, serving as a Rajya Sabha Member of Parliament from 2014 to 2016 before continuing his political journey in later years. Today, he has acted in over 350 films across Hindi, Bengali, Odia, Bhojpuri and several other languages. His remarkable career has been recognised with three National Film Awards, a Filmfare Award, the Padma Bhushan and, most recently, the prestigious Dadasaheb Phalke Award for his contribution to Indian cinema. A legacy built on resilience For many, Mithun Chakraborty will always be remembered for his dance moves, unforgettable songs and larger-than-life screen presence. But his greatest achievement lies beyond the spotlight. More than 350 films, three National Awards and a legendary career later, Mithun’s biggest legacy remains his journey from struggle to stardom. Photograph: ( Wikimedia Commons ) From leaving behind a troubled past and enduring poverty in Mumbai to becoming one of India's most celebrated actors, Mithun's life is a reminder that setbacks don't define the future. Reinvention is always possible for those willing to keep going. Decades after he first stepped onto the silver screen, his journey continues to inspire, not only because of the fame he achieved, but because of the determination that carried him through every phase of his career. Source: ‘Bollywood superstar, worth Rs 400 crore, once slept on footpaths, was disrespected for his skin colour. Today, he owns over 16 bungalows’ by The Economic Times, Published on 19 August 2025.
